request.question: KOIL ran a radio show featuring Mark Levin who worked for which Attorney General?

title: George J. Terwilliger III
Mark Levin
WHLO
The Liberty Amendments
WNIX
Earth 2 (TV series)
KOIL

sentences: George J. Terwilliger III (born June 5, 1950) is an American lawyer and politician.
He is a partner in the Washington, D.C. office of McGuireWoods LLP and is a former United States Deputy Attorney General and acting United States Attorney General.
Terwilliger, of Vermont, was nominated on February 14, 1992, by President George H.W. Bush to be Deputy Attorney General at the U.S. Department of Justice.
He would succeed William Pelham Barr.

As Deputy Attorney General, Terwilliger became the second-highest-ranking official in the United States Department of Justice (DOJ) and ran the day-to-day operations of the Department, serving in that position from 1991 through 1993.
He was appointed to the position after serving as the United States Attorney for the District of Vermont.
In addition, he currently serves on the Advisory Board of Intellaine, LLP, a U.S. defense and risk engineering firm located in Arlington, Virginia.
Mark Reed Levin ( ; born September 21, 1957) is an American lawyer, author, and radio personality.
He is the host of syndicated radio show "The Mark Levin Show".
Levin worked in the administration of President Ronald Reagan and was a chief of staff for Attorney General Edwin Meese.
He is president of the Landmark Legal Foundation, has authored seven books, and contributes commentary to various media outlets such as "National Review Online".
On September 1, 2015, Levin was named Editor-in-Chief of Conservative Review.
WHLO (640 AM) – branded 640 WHLO – is a commercial radio station licensed to Akron, Ohio, serving both the Akron and Canton metro areas.
Owned by iHeartMedia, the station broadcasts primarily a news/talk format.

WHLO is the local affiliate for Fox News Radio; conservative talk programs "The Rush Limbaugh Show", "The Sean Hannity Show", "The Mark Levin Show", and "The Schnitt Show"; and overnight night talk shows "The Dave Ramsey Show" and "Coast to Coast AM".
The station also serves as the radio home of the Kent State Golden Flashes.
The WHLO studios are located in North Canton, while the station transmitter resides in Copley.
In addition to a standard analog transmission, WHLO is available online via iHeartRadio.
The Liberty Amendments: Restoring the American Republic is a book by the American talk radio host and lawyer Mark Levin, published in 2013.
In it, Levin lays out and makes a case for eleven Constitutional amendments which he believes would restore the Constitution’s chief components: federalism, republicanism, and limited government.
WNIX (1330 AM) is a radio station broadcasting a News-Talk format, featuring Rush Limbaugh, Michael Savage, Mark Levin, Doug Stephan and Dave Ramsey.
Licensed to Greenville, Mississippi, USA, the station is currently owned by Delta Radio Network LLC.
Earth 2 is an American science fiction television series which aired on NBC from November 6, 1994 to June 4, 1995.
The show was canceled after one season of 22 episodes.

It follows the journey and settlement of a small expeditionary group called the Eden Project, with the intent to journey to an Earth-like planet called G889 in an attempt to find a cure to an illness called "the syndrome".

The series was created by Michael Duggan, Carol Flint, Mark Levin, and Billy Ray, produced by Amblin Entertainment and Universal Television, and filmed primarily in northern New Mexico around the Santa Fe area.
The series' music was composed by David Bergeaud, and the executive producers were Michael Duggan, Mark Levin, and Carol Flint.
KOIL (1290 kHz) is a commercial AM radio station, licensed to Omaha, Nebraska.
It is owned by NRG Media (headquartered in Cedar Rapids, Iowa) and airs a Talk radio format.
KOIL's weekday schedule is mostly nationally syndicated talk shows such as Don Imus, Laura Ingraham, Sean Hannity, Mark Levin, Jim Bohannon, Clyde Lewis and Jerry Doyle.
It also airs games from the Omaha Lancers junior ice hockey team.
KOIL's studios are located at Dodge Street and 50th Avenue in Midtown Omaha, and its transmitter site is located in Bellevue, Nebraska.
KOIL operates at 5000 watts around the clock but at night it uses a directional antenna to protect other stations on 1290 kHz.
WWLZ (820 AM) is a radio station in the Elmira-Corning market in New York state.

It is a talk radio station that airs the Premiere Networks lineup ("Glenn Beck Radio Program", "The Rush Limbaugh Show", "The Sean Hannity Show" and "Coast to Coast AM") during the workday and overnight, Cumulus Media Networks programs ("First Light", "Imus in the Morning", "The Mark Levin Show", "The John Batchelor Show") during the evening and in morning drive, MRN races and Elmira Jackals hockey.
WTMA (branded as News Talk 1250 WTMA) is an AM radio station licensed to Charleston, South Carolina with a Talk radio format.
This station operates on 1250 kHz and is under the ownership of Cumulus Media.
WTMA airs a local news and interview morning show hosted by Charlie James.
The rest of the weekday schedule is made up of nationally syndicated talk shows from Cumulus subsidiary Westwood One Networks.
They include Chris Plante, Clark Howard, Michael Savage, Mark Levin, John Batchelor, Red Eye Radio and First Light.
Weekends feature shows on money, health, real estate and gardening, with syndicated shows from Larry Kudlow, Bob Brinker and repeats of weekday shows.
Each hour begins with Westwood One News.
The Mark Levin Show is a conservative talk radio show hosted by Mark Levin.
The program is broadcast nationwide on Westwood One and reaches an estimated seven million weekly listeners, according to an estimate from "Talkers Magazine".

The "Talkers" estimate puts Levin's listenership in a tie with "The Glenn Beck Radio Program" for fourth most-listened-to talk radio show in the United States and, counting all radio formats, tied for ninth most-listened-to radio program in the United States.

candidate.summarize1.predict:
Given the fields `question` and `passages`, produce the single field `summary`.

Goal: compress the grounded first-hop evidence into a short intermediate state that makes it easy for the next-hop agent to (a) see what facts are present per candidate/entity, (b) see what is ambiguous or missing, and (c) know 1–4 prioritized next-hop retrieval targets or concise queries. This module does not produce the final answer unless the provided passages already unambiguously contain every fact required to resolve the question's relation for every argument.

Procedure:
1) Decide whether the question's relation is semantically complete given the supplied passages. Treat the hop as INCOMPLETE if any of the following hold: a) the relation requires combining or comparing facts across pages (e.g., comparing birth dates, publication/founding years, ordinal ranks, confirming a named director among candidates) and the passages do not supply each required fact unambiguously for all relevant arguments; b) any relation argument is ambiguous or has multiple candidate pages or mentions in the passages; c) an expected candidate page or explicit fact for an argument is missing (e.g., only one side of a comparison is present). Do not trigger INCOMPLETE solely from surface tokens (years, digits) — base the decision on whether the relation's arguments are unambiguously resolved.

2) If the hop is COMPLETE (all required facts to resolve the relation for every argument are present unambiguously in the passages): produce a concise derived result in `summary`. This may be a short phrase or sentence that states the necessary fact(s) needed by the pipeline. Keep it minimal and grounded in the passages; you may include a 1–2 token citation (passage title) if helpful.

3) If the hop is INCOMPLETE or AMBIGUOUS: do NOT assert a final answer or perform the final comparison/selection. Instead emit a short structured report in `summary` containing these labeled elements (prefer bullets or a compact paragraph with explicit labels). Keep all text neutral and non-assertive.

  - evidence: For each named candidate/argument mentioned in the question or passages, list the candidate label and the exact passage title(s) that mention it plus a very short quoted snippet or explicit fact token found there (1–12 words). If a candidate is not mentioned, say so briefly.

  - ambiguities_or_missing: Explicitly name any ambiguities (e.g., multiple candidates for the same name, multiple pages that could be the intended entity) and any missing expected pages or facts.

  - missing_facts_or_bridge: State exactly which fact(s) or disambiguation is needed to complete the relation (e.g., "birth date for Person A", "publication year of Work B", "director credit for Film C"). Be as specific and minimal as possible.

  - retrieval_direction: Provide 1–4 prioritized next-hop targets or concise queries to resolve the missing facts/ambiguities. Prefer explicit page titles when available (exact if known), otherwise provide short search-style queries (few words) or a clear description of the document to retrieve. Order targets by priority.

Formatting and style constraints (apply to both COMPLETE and INCOMPLETE cases):
- The output must be a single `summary` field only. Do not emit other fields.
- In INCOMPLETE reports, use neutral, non-assertive phrasing. Do not produce final comparisons, numeric answers, or definitive selections (e.g., avoid "X is the oldest" or "Answer: 3").
- Keep the report compact and actionable (short labeled bullets or one concise paragraph). Avoid long prose.
- It is allowed and encouraged to quote short factual snippets from passages and cite passage titles to show grounding.
- Do not defer or mark INCOMPLETE when the passages already unambiguously provide all facts for the relation and its arguments.

Produce the `summary` content that follows these rules.
candidate.create_query_hop2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, produce the single field `query`.

Goal
- Produce exactly one compact, retrieval-intent query suitable for a second-hop search that will recover the missing bridging evidence needed to answer the question. Do not produce the final answer in `query`.

Semantic decision rule (when to emit a retrieval-intent query)
- If summary_1 does NOT assert a confirmed linking entity or verified bridging fact needed to answer the question, you MUST emit exactly one compact retrieval-intent query. "Does not assert a confirmed bridge" includes short or candidate-only summaries (e.g., a single named entity, a quoted candidate title, or numeric anchors that are not verified) and any case where the visible anchors do not unambiguously identify the needed bridge.
- If summary_1 already names a confirmed bridge (a clearly identified, verified linking entity or unambiguous metadata that directly connects the question's pieces), you may produce a compact query to fetch supporting evidence, but do NOT produce an answer in the `query` field.
- If summary_1 explicitly states that the bridge cannot be recovered automatically (extreme ambiguity or user-local context requiring clarification), do NOT force a misleading retrieval query. In that case, set `query` to the single short token BRIDGE_UNRECOVERABLE (all caps, no punctuation) so downstream steps can prompt the user for clarification. Do not invent facts.

Query construction rules
- Output exactly one compact line of text as `query`. Keep it short and retrieval-focused (few keywords, no full sentences). Use visible anchors from `question` and `summary_1` and minimal retrieval keywords that express the missing relation (e.g., anchor1 anchor2 which artist recorded both; AlbumTitle album artist genre; 1991–92 NHL season regular season).
- Do not include the final answer, assertions, or user-facing instructions in the `query` field. Avoid polite phrasing, questions to the user, or explanatory text. Prefer noun phrases and relation keywords.
- If your natural impulse would be to write a declarative answer or a clarification sentence in `query`, instead convert that output into a compact retrieval-intent query that targets the missing bridge.
- Preserve semantic fidelity: base the query only on visible anchors and relations in `question` and `summary_1`. Do not invent hidden identifiers or assume unseen facts.

Format contract
- Return exactly one field named `query` whose value is a single short string. If unrecoverable as described above, set `query` to BRIDGE_UNRECOVERABLE.

Examples of disallowed outputs for `query`
- A declarative answer (e.g., "The artist was X.")
- A user-facing clarification sentence (e.g., "Do you mean X or Y?") unless summary_1 explicitly requires clarification — in that case use BRIDGE_UNRECOVERABLE.

Behavioral constraints
- Do not use surface heuristics (token count, punctuation, or quotation marks) as the trigger; apply the semantic rule above.
- Emit only one compact retrieval string when the bridge is missing; do not emit multiple queries or long explanations.

Inputs available: `question`, `summary_1`. Output required: single field `query`.
candidate.summarize2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `context`, `passages`, produce the fields `summary`. The `summary` must be a concise, post-retrieval integration of the evidence that preserves the facts needed by the final answer. Follow these rules when composing `summary`:

- Purpose: Integrate the first-hop `context` with the newly retrieved `passages` into a grounded state that retains or resolves the earlier bridge only when the second-hop passages supply relation- or attribute-facts that are necessary to answer or disambiguate the `question` (for example: partnership/membership links, joint-award partners indicated by 'with', or requested biographical qualifiers such as nationality, birthdate, current team/status).

- When to append supporting sentence(s): Only append short passage-grounded supporting sentence(s) when the question's semantic intent requires relation or attribute facts to answer or disambiguate. In those cases, produce a compact integrated summary that (a) preserves the concise primary answer or canonical label/token chosen from `context`, and (b) appends one short supporting sentence per relevant entity (or one joint sentence for explicit partnerships) that is directly supported by the supplied `passages` and identity-aligned to the entity referenced in `context`.

- When not to append: Do not append supporting sentences and instead return only the canonical label/token when any of the following apply: the question expects a normalized identifier or single token (examples: binary/multiple-choice label selection between named options, identification-only questions, or explicit token-extraction such as 'middle name'); or the `context` already contains a full, passage-consistent, multi-clause integrated answer. These are semantic exceptions — do not append merely because an answer in `context` is short.

- Identity alignment and verification: Before appending, confirm that the fact in `passages` refers to the same entity referenced in `context` (match names, common aliases, roles, dates, or other corroborating attributes). If identity is ambiguous or unsupported, refrain from appending and preserve uncertainty instead.

- Style and content constraints: Keep output concise. If appending, limit `summary` to 1–3 sentences total (the primary concise answer plus at most one short supporting sentence per relevant entity or one joint supporting sentence). Avoid inventing facts, avoid long verbatim quotations from `passages`, and avoid name-collision errors. Do not change which label or name is selected by the existing answer-selection logic.

- If there is insufficient information in `context` and `passages` to resolve the needed relation/attribute, summarize the uncertainty succinctly rather than asserting unsupported facts.

Produce only the `summary` field as the integrated output string.
candidate.final_answer.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, `summary_2`, produce the fields `answer`.

Use only summary_1 and summary_2 as evidence. Treat summary_2 as the later, integrated evidence and prioritize it when reconciling information; use summary_1 only as the earlier, pre-second-hop context. Do not assume the final state is unresolved solely because summary_1 contains bridging or retrieval-oriented language.

Produce exactly one field, answer, whose value is the minimal final answer string that is fully supported by the visible summaries. The answer field must contain only that minimal plain-text string and nothing else (no explanation, no surrounding quotes, no JSON wrappers, no debugging or transport artifacts).

Decide whether the question's intent is binary (a truth-value / yes-no question) or non-binary (asking for a name, title, date, place, numeric count, selection, or other factual value). Make this decision semantically (based on the question's meaning), not by surface heuristics like token counts or punctuation.

- If the question's intent is binary AND both summary_1 and summary_2 unambiguously agree on the same truth-value, emit exactly one lowercase token: either yes or no (and nothing else) as the answer value.

- For all other question intents (non-binary), emit the minimal factual string supported by the summaries exactly as needed: preserve multi-token values, necessary punctuation, full date formats, and place formats (for example, full names, "City, State", or "Month Day, Year"), and do not truncate these to a single token.

If the summaries conflict, are ambiguous, or do not jointly support an unambiguous minimal answer, do not fabricate or over-assert; follow the system's established fallback behavior for ambiguous or unsupported questions.

Always forbid emitting serialized queries, JSON/dict-like structures, internal-debug wrappers, or other transport artifacts as the final answer. The answer must be plain text only.

Make all judgments about minimality and agreement based on the semantic content of summary_1 and summary_2, with priority to summary_2 when they differ.

Produce the answer value exactly as specified above in the answer field.

sentences: George J. Terwilliger III (born June 5, 1950) is an American lawyer and politician.
He is a partner in the Washington, D.C. office of McGuireWoods LLP and is a former United States Deputy Attorney General and acting United States Attorney General.
Terwilliger, of Vermont, was nominated on February 14, 1992, by President George H.W. Bush to be Deputy Attorney General at the U.S. Department of Justice.
He would succeed William Pelham Barr.

As Deputy Attorney General, Terwilliger became the second-highest-ranking official in the United States Department of Justice (DOJ) and ran the day-to-day operations of the Department, serving in that position from 1991 through 1993.
He was appointed to the position after serving as the United States Attorney for the District of Vermont.
In addition, he currently serves on the Advisory Board of Intellaine, LLP, a U.S. defense and risk engineering firm located in Arlington, Virginia.
Mark Reed Levin ( ; born September 21, 1957) is an American lawyer, author, and radio personality.
He is the host of syndicated radio show "The Mark Levin Show".
Levin worked in the administration of President Ronald Reagan and was a chief of staff for Attorney General Edwin Meese.
He is president of the Landmark Legal Foundation, has authored seven books, and contributes commentary to various media outlets such as "National Review Online".
On September 1, 2015, Levin was named Editor-in-Chief of Conservative Review.

Owned by iHeartMedia, the station broadcasts primarily a news/talk format.

WHLO is the local affiliate for Fox News Radio; conservative talk programs "The Rush Limbaugh Show", "The Sean Hannity Show", "The Mark Levin Show", and "The Schnitt Show"; and overnight night talk shows "The Dave Ramsey Show" and "Coast to Coast AM".
The station also serves as the radio home of the Kent State Golden Flashes.
The WHLO studios are located in North Canton, while the station transmitter resides in Copley.
In addition to a standard analog transmission, WHLO is available online via iHeartRadio.
The Liberty Amendments: Restoring the American Republic is a book by the American talk radio host and lawyer Mark Levin, published in 2013.
In it, Levin lays out and makes a case for eleven Constitutional amendments which he believes would restore the Constitution’s chief components: federalism, republicanism, and limited government.
WNIX (1330 AM) is a radio station broadcasting a News-Talk format, featuring Rush Limbaugh, Michael Savage, Mark Levin, Doug Stephan and Dave Ramsey.
Licensed to Greenville, Mississippi, USA, the station is currently owned by Delta Radio Network LLC.
Earth 2 is an American science fiction television series which aired on NBC from November 6, 1994 to June 4, 1995.
The show was canceled after one season of 22 episodes.

It follows the journey and settlement of a small expeditionary group called the Eden Project, with the intent to journey to an Earth-like planet called G889 in an attempt to find a cure to an illness called "the syndrome".

The series was created by Michael Duggan, Carol Flint, Mark Levin, and Billy Ray, produced by Amblin Entertainment and Universal Television, and filmed primarily in northern New Mexico around the Santa Fe area.
The series' music was composed by David Bergeaud, and the executive producers were Michael Duggan, Mark Levin, and Carol Flint.
KOIL (1290 kHz) is a commercial AM radio station, licensed to Omaha, Nebraska.
It is owned by NRG Media (headquartered in Cedar Rapids, Iowa) and airs a Talk radio format.
KOIL's weekday schedule is mostly nationally syndicated talk shows such as Don Imus, Laura Ingraham, Sean Hannity, Mark Levin, Jim Bohannon, Clyde Lewis and Jerry Doyle.
It also airs games from the Omaha Lancers junior ice hockey team.
KOIL's studios are located at Dodge Street and 50th Avenue in Midtown Omaha, and its transmitter site is located in Bellevue, Nebraska.
KOIL operates at 5000 watts around the clock but at night it uses a directional antenna to protect other stations on 1290 kHz.
WWLZ (820 AM) is a radio station in the Elmira-Corning market in New York state.

It is a talk radio station that airs the Premiere Networks lineup ("Glenn Beck Radio Program", "The Rush Limbaugh Show", "The Sean Hannity Show" and "Coast to Coast AM") during the workday and overnight, Cumulus Media Networks programs ("First Light", "Imus in the Morning", "The Mark Levin Show", "The John Batchelor Show") during the evening and in morning drive, MRN races and Elmira Jackals hockey.
WTMA (branded as News Talk 1250 WTMA) is an AM radio station licensed to Charleston, South Carolina with a Talk radio format.
This station operates on 1250 kHz and is under the ownership of Cumulus Media.
WTMA airs a local news and interview morning show hosted by Charlie James.
The rest of the weekday schedule is made up of nationally syndicated talk shows from Cumulus subsidiary Westwood One Networks.
They include Chris Plante, Clark Howard, Michael Savage, Mark Levin, John Batchelor, Red Eye Radio and First Light.
Weekends feature shows on money, health, real estate and gardening, with syndicated shows from Larry Kudlow, Bob Brinker and repeats of weekday shows.
Each hour begins with Westwood One News.
The Mark Levin Show is a conservative talk radio show hosted by Mark Levin.
The program is broadcast nationwide on Westwood One and reaches an estimated seven million weekly listeners, according to an estimate from "Talkers Magazine".

The "Talkers" estimate puts Levin's listenership in a tie with "The Glenn Beck Radio Program" for fourth most-listened-to talk radio show in the United States and, counting all radio formats, tied for ninth most-listened-to radio program in the United States.

He is president of the Landmark Legal Foundation, has authored seven books, and contributes commentary to various media outlets such as "National Review Online".  On September 1, 2015, Levin was named Editor-in-Chief of Conservative Review.

KOIL's weekday schedule is mostly nationally syndicated talk shows such as Don Imus, Laura Ingraham, Sean Hannity, Mark Levin, Jim Bohannon, Clyde Lewis and Jerry Doyle.  It also airs games from the Omaha Lancers junior ice hockey team. The Mark Levin Show | The Mark Levin Show is a conservative talk radio show hosted by Mark Levin.  The program is broadcast nationwide on Westwood One and reaches an estimated seven million weekly listeners, according to an estimate from "Talkers Magazine".  The "Talkers" estimate puts Levin's listenership in a tie with "The Glenn Beck Radio Program" for fourth most-listened-to talk radio show in the United States and, counting all radio formats, tied for ninth most-listened-to radio program in the United States.

Mark Levine (journalist) | Mark H. Levine (born May 7, 1966) is the Democratic Delegate to the Virginia House of Delegates from Virginia's 45th District, which encompasses the eastern half of the City of Alexandria, some of the northern West End, parts of South Arlington, and the Alexandria portion of Fairfax County.  A Constitutional lawyer, Levine served as Legislative Counsel to Congressman Barney Frank (D-MA) from 2001 to 2003 and was an early advocate in the "Marriage Equality” movement.  Since 2003, Levine has hosted a nationally syndicated progressive public policy radio program and worked as a TV pundit.  In 2014, Levine ran in the Democratic Primary for Virginia's 8th Congressional District last year, a seat currently held by Rep. Don Beyer.

WCHM | WCHM (1490 AM) and 96.7 FM is a radio station broadcasting a News Talk Information format.  Licensed to Clarkesville, Georgia, USA.  The station is currently owned by WCHM Radio, LLC, an affiliated company of Batten Communications, inc. and features programming from Premiere Radio Networks and FOX News Radio.  Major shows include Rush Limbaugh, Glenn Beck, Mark Levin, Sean Hannity, Clark Howard, Michael Savage and Coast to Coast with George Noory.  The station also features local programming and University of Georgia sports programming.

Ketzel Levine | Ketzel Levine is an American radio journalist who began her broadcast career in 1974.  She joined National Public Radio (NPR) in 1977 and worked, variously, as the network's arts producer, sports director, features reporter and garden expert.  From 2000 through 2008, she was senior correspondent for the NPR program "Morning Edition".  At the end of that year, due to cutbacks at the network, Levine was laid off, while working on a documentary series about Americans coping with economic stress and job loss.  Her final NPR broadcast was about how she, herself, had just lost her job.

WTMA | WTMA (branded as News Talk 1250 WTMA) is an AM radio station licensed to Charleston, South Carolina with a Talk radio format.  This station operates on 1250 kHz and is under the ownership of Cumulus Media.  WTMA airs a local news and interview morning show hosted by Charlie James.  The rest of the weekday schedule is made up of nationally syndicated talk shows from Cumulus subsidiary Westwood One Networks.  They include Chris Plante, Clark Howard, Michael Savage, Mark Levin, John Batchelor, Red Eye Radio and First Light.  Weekends feature shows on money, health, real estate and gardening, with syndicated shows from Larry Kudlow, Bob Brinker and repeats of weekday shows.  Each hour begins with Westwood One News.

response.hop2_query: Mark Levin chief of staff Edwin Meese
hop2_docs: Mark Levin | Mark Reed Levin ( ; born September 21, 1957) is an American lawyer, author, and radio personality.  He is the host of syndicated radio show "The Mark Levin Show".  Levin worked in the administration of President Ronald Reagan and was a chief of staff for Attorney General Edwin Meese.  He is president of the Landmark Legal Foundation, has authored seven books, and contributes commentary to various media outlets such as "National Review Online".  On September 1, 2015, Levin was named Editor-in-Chief of Conservative Review.

Meese Report | The final report of the Attorney General's Commission on Pornography (usually referred to as (the) Meese Report, for U.S. Attorney General Edwin Meese) is the result of a comprehensive investigation into pornography ordered by U.S. President Ronald Reagan.  It was published in July 1986 and contains 1,960 pages.

Edwin Meese | Edwin Meese III (born December 2, 1931) is an American attorney, law professor, author and member of the Republican Party who served in official capacities within the Ronald Reagan Gubernatorial Administration (1967–1974), the Reagan Presidential Transition Team (1980) and the Reagan White House (1981–1985), eventually rising to hold the position of the 75th Attorney General of the United States (1985–1988).

Arnold Burns | Arnold Irwin Burns (April 14, 1930 – October 1, 2013) was an American lawyer.  He served as the United States Deputy Attorney General from 1986 to 1988 under President Ronald Reagan and U.S. Attorney General Edwin Meese.  In March 1988, Burns, together with the head of the U.S. Justice Department's criminal division William Weld and four aides, resigned from office in protest of what they viewed as improper conduct by Attorney General Meese, including personal financial indiscretions.  In July 1988, Burns and Weld jointly testified before the U.S. Congress in support of a potential prosecution of Meese following an investigation by a special prosecutor, who had declined to file charges.  Meese resigned from office later in July 1988, shortly after Burns and Weld appeared before Congress.

Edwin Bramall | Field Marshal Edwin Noel Westby Bramall, Baron Bramall, {'1': ", '2': ", '3': ", '4': "} (born 18 December 1923) is a retired senior British Army officer.  He served as Chief of the General Staff, the professional head of the British Army, between 1979 and 1982, and as Chief of the Defence Staff, professional head of the British Armed Forces, from 1982 to 1985.  He developed the concept of the "Fifth Pillar" pulling together the activities of defence attachés to form a structure for intervention in smaller countries.

Blair Levin | Blair Steven Levin is an American lawyer formerly with the Federal Communications Commission, who served as the Executive Director of the National Broadband Plan from 2009 to 2010.  During the Presidency of Bill Clinton he was chief of staff to FCC chairman Reed Hundt from 1993 to 1997.

Maryland Heights, Missouri | Maryland Heights is a second-ring west-central suburb of St. Louis, located in St. Louis County, Missouri, United States.  The population was 27,472 at the 2010 census.  The city was incorporated in 1985.  Edwin L. Dirck was elected the city's first mayor.  Mark M. Levin served as City Administrator from August 1985 to 2015.  Mr. Levin is now an associate professor at Indiana University Bloomington.
